perturbation.original_text: For a period of five (5) years from the date of receiving it, all Confidential Information will be maintained in confidence by the receiving party, will not be disclosed to any third party or to any persons employed in its business other than those having a need to know for the purposes set forth above, and will be protected with the same degree of care as the receiving party normally uses in the protection of its own confidential and proprietary information, but in no case with any less degree than reasonable care.
perturbation.changed_text: For a period of five (5) years from the date of receiving it, all Confidential Information will be maintained in confidence by the receiving party, will not be disclosed to any third party or to any persons employed in its business other than those having a perceived need to know, and will be protected with a level of care that the receiving party believes is adequate.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies 'a need to know for the purposes set forth above' creating a clear obligation. The modified text changes 'need to know' to 'perceived need to know' and replaces the detailed care standard with 'a level of care that the receiving party believes is adequate.' This introduces subjectivity and vagueness, weakening the obligation to protect confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ontario Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA)
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA Principle 4.7 states that personal information shall be protected by security safeguards appropriate to the sensitivity of the information. The modified text's 'level of care that the receiving party believes is adequate' introduces subjectivity and does not guarantee safeguards 'appropriate to the sensitivity of the information', potentially violating PIPEDA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act Skip to main content Skip to "About this site" Skip to section menu Assented to 2000-04-13 An Act to support and promote electronic commerce by protecting personal information that is collected, used or disclosed in certain circumstances, by providing for the use of electronic means to communicate or record information or transactions and by amending the Canada Evidence Act, the Statutory Instruments Act and the Statute Revision Act Her Majesty, by and with the advice and consent of the Senate and House of Commons of Canada, enacts as follows: Short Title Marginal note: Short title 1 Â This Act may be cited as the Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act . PART 1 Protection of Personal Information in the Private Sector Interpretation Marginal note: Definitions 2 Â (1) Â The definitions in this subsection apply in this Part. alternative format alternative format , with respect to personal information, means a format that allows a person with a sensory disability to read or listen to the personal information.â( support de substitution ) breach of security safeguards breach of security safeguards âmeans the loss of, unauthorized access to or unauthorized disclosure of personal information resulting from a breach of an organizationâs security safeguards that are referred to in clause 4.7 of Schedule 1 or from a failure to establish those safeguards.â( atteinte aux mesures de sÃ©curitÃ© ) business contact information business contact information âmeans any information that is used for the purpose of communicating or facilitating communication with an individual in relation to their employment, business or profession such as the individualâs name, position name or title, work address, work telephone number, work fax number or work electronic address.â( coordonnÃ©es dâaffaires ) business transaction business transaction âincludes (a) Â the purchase, sale or other acquisition or disposition of an organization or a part of an organization, or any of its assets; (b) Â the merger or amalgamation of two or more organizations; (c) Â the making of a loan or provision of other financing to an organization or a part of an organization; (d) Â the creating of a charge on, or the taking of a security interest in or a security on, any assets or securities of an organization; (e) Â the lease or licensing of any of an organizationâs assets; and (f) Â any other prescribed arrangement between two or more organizations to conduct a business activity.â( transaction commerciale ) commercial activity commercial activity âmeans any particular transaction, act or conduct or any regular course of conduct that is of a commercial character, including the selling, bartering or leasing of donor, membership or other fundraising lists.â( activitÃ© commerciale ) Commissioner Commissioner âmeans the Privacy Commissioner appointed under section 53 of the Privacy Act .â( commissaire ) Court Court âmeans the Federal Court.â( Cour ) federal work, undertaking or business federal work, undertaking or business âmeans any work, undertaking or business that is within the legislative authority of Parliament. It includes (a) Â a work, undertaking or business that is operated or carried on for or in connection with navigation and shipping, whether inland or maritim
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information by changing 'need to know for the purposes set forth above' to 'perceived need to know' and replacing the detailed care standard with 'a level of care that the receiving party believes is adequate.' This introduces subjectivity. The Law Explanation states this 'introduces subjectivity and does not guarantee safeguards 'appropriate to the sensitivity of the information', potentially violating PIPEDA.' Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'breach of security safeguards' as 'the loss of, unauthorized access to or unauthorized disclosure of personal information resulting from a breach of an organizationâs security safeguards that are referred to in clause 4.7 of Schedule 1 or from a failure to establish those safeguards.' By weakening the obligation to protect confidential information, the modified text increases the risk of a breach of security safeguards, contradicting the intent of PIPEDA.

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall retain all rights of ownership over all intellectual property associated with the above referenced subject matter , including the rights of ownership of patents, trademarks and copyrights.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall generally retain rights of ownership over intellectual property related to the subject matter, which may include patents, trademarks, and copyrights, as deemed appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'generally', 'may include', and 'as deemed appropriate,' the original statement is weakened and becomes less definitive. The original stated parties 'shall retain all rights' and this now indicates that they may or may not, at their discretion.
perturbation.law_citation: Copyright Act, R.S.C., 1985, c. C-42, s. 13
perturbation.law_explanation: The Canadian Copyright Act provides automatic copyright protection to original works. By stating that rights 'may include' copyrights 'as deemed appropriate,' this suggests copyright ownership is discretionary, which directly contradicts the automatic nature of copyright as established by the Copyright Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: e person by whom the author was employed shall, in the absence of any agreement to the contrary, be the first owner of the copyright, but where the work is an article or other contribution to a newspaper, magazine or similar periodical, there shall, in the absence of any agreement to the contrary, be deemed to be reserved to the author a right to restrain the publication of the work, otherwise than as part of a newspaper, magazine or similar periodical. Marginal note: Assignments and licences (4) Â The owner of the copyright in any work may assign the right, either wholly or partially, and either generally or subject to limitations relating to territory, medium or sector of the market or other limitations relating to the scope of the assignment, and either for the whole term of the copyright or for any other part thereof, and may grant any interest in the right by licence, but no assignment or grant is valid unless it is in writing signed by the owner of the right in respect of which the assignment or grant is made, or by the ownerâs duly authorized agent. Marginal note: Ownership in case of partial assignment (5) Â Where, under any partial assignment of copyright, the assignee becomes entitled to any right comprised in copyright, the assignee, with respect to the rights so assigned, and the assignor, with respect to the rights not assigned, shall be treated for the purposes of this Act as the owner of the copyright, and this Act has effect accordingly. Marginal note: Assignment of right of action (6) Â For greater certainty, it is deemed always to have been the law that a right of action for infringement of copyright may be assigned in association with the assignment of the copyright or the grant of an interest in the copyright by licence. Marginal note: Exclusive licence (7) Â For greater certainty, it is deemed always to have been the law that a grant of an exclusive licence in a copyright constitutes the grant of an interest in the copyright by licence. R.S., 1985, c. perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall retain all rights' to a discretionary inclusion of rights such as copyrights 'as deemed appropriate' directly undermines the automatic copyright protection as per the Law Explanation. While the provided snippet from the Canadian Copyright Act focuses on ownership in employment contexts and assignment/licensing, the Law Explanation emphasizes the automatic nature of copyright. The modified text's discretionary language contradicts this automaticity, creating a legal contradiction under the Ambiguity Legal Contradiction definition, as it introduces ambiguity that makes the enforcement of copyright difficult. The term 'generally' weakens the obligation, and the phrase 'as deemed appropriate' implies rights are not automatically retained, but subject to discretion, thus undermining legal compliance.

perturbation.original_text: Neither party shall not, and it shall ensure that a ny of its affiliates do not, for a period of five years from the date of this Agreement, without the prior written consent of the other party, induce any employee employed by th e other party to leave such employ or offer to employ or employ such employee.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither party should actively encourage, nor should they permit their affiliates to actively encourage, for a period of five years from the date of this Agreement, without the expressed consent of the other party, any employee employed by the other party to consider alternative employment opportunities.
perturbation.explanation: The original text prohibits inducing or employing the other party's employees. The modified version replaces 'shall not' with 'should actively encourage', weakening the prohibition. It replaces 'induce any employee...to leave such employ or offer to employ or employ such employee' with 'encourage any employee...to consider alternative employment opportunities' which has a much weaker and more ambiguous scope. These changes make it less clear what constitutes a violation, as 'consider alternative employment opportunities' is more benign than directly poaching an employee.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law - Tortious Interference with Contractual Relations
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a statute, the common law tort of interference with contractual relations protects against unlawful interference with another's contractual rights. The original clause provides a clear contractual protection against employee poaching. The modified language, by weakening the prohibition and focusing on 'considering alternative employment opportunities,' makes it more difficult to prove the tort of interference, as it is harder to demonstrate active inducement to breach the employment contract, potentially undermining the protection afforded by the common law.

Accept and close Share this: Facebook Twitter Reddit LinkedIn WhatsApp Legal Case Summary Lumley v Gye [1853] 2 E & B 216 Tort – Wrongdoing – cause of action against third party Facts Miss Wagner was an opera singer at Lumley’s theatre on a three month contract. Gye and Wagner made an agreement that Wagner would break her contract and refuse to sing at Lumley’s theatre. Wagner agreed to sing at Gye’s theatre only. Issue Whether Lumley had a cause of action against a third party for wrongdoing and whether he was entitled to claim damages. Decision / Outcome Wagner and Gye were joint wrongdoers as they conspired together, causing loss. Where a third party interferes, either intentionally and or maliciously with the performance of the contract, they are liable for any loss (in the form of damages) that arises from the interference, either individually or jointly. Any persuasion used to influence a contracting party to breach their contractual obligations could go towards proving the third party demonstrated an intention to interfere and cause loss. The court held, there were two propositions that determined whether a third party could be liable for an actionable tort. Either, the person whose persuasions to break the contract was liable, or that the action of seducing a person from a contract, or persuading a person who has contracted for service was found liable for damages for their interference. Thus, it was deemed that the application was wide enough to encompass the current case where it would apply to an ope
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original contractual protection against employee poaching, making it more difficult to prove tortious interference. The original text established a clear prohibition against inducing or employing the other party's employees. However, the changed text replaces 'shall not' with 'should actively encourage', reducing the force of the prohibition. It also substitutes 'induce any employee...to leave such employ or offer to employ or employ such employee' with 'encourage any employee...to consider alternative employment opportunities', which has a more ambiguous and less restrictive scope. This aligns with the Perturbation Explanation, which highlights that the change weakens the prohibition and makes violations less clear. Law Snippet 2, discussing Lumley v Gye, states that 'Any persuasion used to influence a contracting party to breach their contractual obligations could go towards proving the third party demonstrated an intention to interfere and cause loss.' The modified language, by only prohibiting the encouragement of 'considering alternative employment opportunities', could be argued to fall outside the scope of 'persuasion...to breach their contractual obligations,' thus weakening the protection afforded by the original clause and creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the laws of the Province of Ontario.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be generally guided by principles similar to the laws of the Province of Ontario.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause clearly states that Ontario law governs the agreement. The modified clause replaces 'governed by and interpreted in accordance with' with 'generally guided by principles similar to', removing the binding force of Ontario law and introducing ambiguity. It is now unclear which specific laws apply or how they should be interpreted.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ontario Law - Rules of Private International Law (Conflict of Laws)
perturbation.law_explanation: Ontario's conflict of laws rules determine which jurisdiction's laws apply to a contract when there is a choice of law provision. By weakening the choice of law clause to merely being 'generally guided by principles similar to' Ontario law, the modification creates uncertainty as to which legal system governs the contract, potentially conflicting with the established legal principles of determining jurisdiction in contractual disputes under Ontario law. If Ontario law is not explicitly selected, other jurisdictions' laws may inadvertently govern the agreement.

perturbation.justification: The original text stipulates that Ontario law governs the agreement, while the changed text only states it is 'generally guided by principles similar to' Ontario law. This weakens the binding effect of Ontario law. The Law Explanation supports that this creates uncertainty as to which legal system governs the contract, potentially conflicting with established legal principles. The Perturbation Explanation correctly identifies that it is unclear which specific laws apply or how they should be interpreted. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the combination of Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation clearly indicates that the ambiguity introduced undermines the original binding agreement and may lead to unenforceability. Because it moves from a clear application of the law, to a subjective 'guided by principles', this violates the requirement that legal obligations are enforceable and not open to interpretation.
